query: risk factors for infants born from twin pregnancies

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are racial and ethnic population variations in the prevalence of dizygotic twins, with the risk of 1.3 per 1000 births in Japan to 8 per 1000 births in the United States and Europe. The risk uniquely increases in Nigeria, where dizygotic twins comprise up to 50 per 1000 births. With parity, there seems to be an increased risk for dizygotic twins even when adjusted for maternal age. Maternal family history for twinning adds to the risk, but the paternal history is non-contributory. Some studies have suggested those patients with a body mass index (BMI) of more than 30 kg/m2 have a high risk for dizygotic twins. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding fetal aneuploidy risk assessment, dizygotic twin gestations entail doubling the aneuploidy risk; however, the risk for both affected fetuses is theoretical. For monozygotic twin gestations, the aneuploidy risk is congruent, i.e., either both are affected, or both are healthy; thus, the aneuploidy risks for significant gestation based on maternal age can be utilized. When dichorionic diamniotic twin gestation manifests with gender congruence, a distinction between a monozygotic twin gestation with embryonic splitting in the first three days cannot be excluded; however, the vast majority can be assumed to be dizygotic. Noninvasive prenatal testing for twin gestations is available. Serum screening of NT-sequential and QUAD screening are available. If genetic amniocentesis is pursued, the risk of procedure-related loss is in the range of 3%. Additional technical expertise is required to map the twin gestation before the procedure, especially if they are believed to be dizygotic with the same gender. Discordant results with one affected fetus may indicate a selective fetal reduction, and mapping of the twin gestation at the time with genetic amniocentesis assumes additional relevance. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary factor influencing the occurrence of dizygotic twins is the usage of infertility ovulation induction drugs. Almost one-third of all twin infants born in the United States can be attributed to iatrogenic interventions, including medication use, in vitro fertilization (IVF), ovulation induction, and superovulation with intrauterine insemination. IVF also interestingly increases the risk of embryo cleavage that can generate complications of monozygotic twinning. Maternal age is associated with an increased risk for dizygotic twins. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Monoamniotic twin gestations occur when one embryo splits between 9 and 13 days after fertilization. The fetuses thus share both an amniotic sac and a placenta. if the embryo splits after 13 days, conjoined twins may result. this is a rare subtype of monoamniotic twin pregnancy with an incidence of 1.5 per 100,000 pregnancies. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjoined Twins -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of conjoined twins is one per 50,000 to 200,000 births. [4] The rate of stillbirth is high and is estimated to be around 60 percent. [2] There is no increased risk in future pregnancies. Accurate epidemiology across multiple countries and ethnicities cannot be calculated because the condition is underreported. Conjoined twins are associated with a higher predominance of the female sex. [5] The female to male ratio is 3 to 1. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Twin births account for just over 3% of live births and 97% of multiple births in the United States. In the absence of assisted reproductive technology, dizygotic twins are much more common than monozygotic twins and account for 70% of all twin gestations. [1] It is imperative to determine as early as possible in the pregnancy both the chorionicity (number of placentas) and anmionicity (number of amniotic sacs) of every twin pregnancy, as this is directly related to the incidence of complications, with monochorionic twin pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In pregnancies that are achieved with assisted reproductive techniques, 1.9% are monochorionic and 1/3 of those pregnancies are also monoamniotic. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal anomalies pose a higher risk in monoamniotic twins, reaching levels as elevated as 15-25%, with cardiac anomalies being the most prevalent. It is noteworthy that one or both fetuses may be affected, and in cases where both are impacted, they often exhibit distinct anomalies. The anomalies in monoamniotic twins commonly lack a genetic etiology, as the defects are attributed to the later splitting of the embryo and significant placental anastomosis, which facilitates hemodynamic fluctuations. Merely 4% of anomalies in monoamniotic twins are genetically rooted. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) represents another potential complication in twin pregnancies. In monoamniotic twins, the incidence is approximately 2-4%, typically identified through indicators such as a disproportionately large bladder in one twin and an empty bladder in the other or by Doppler findings. In monochorionic diamniotic twins, the incidence of TTTS increases to 10-15%. For monoamniotic twins with TTTS, the neonatal survival rate is generally low when expectant management is pursued. Attempts to address the condition through laser separation of placental anastomoses have yielded similarly unsatisfactory outcomes. The optimal management strategy for TTTS in monoamniotic twins is still an ongoing challenge that awaits discovery. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Epidemiology -- Multiple Gestations
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple gestations are significantly associated with an increased risk of stillbirth. Compared to singleton pregnancies, twin pregnancies show more than double the stillbirth rate (1.15% vs 0.52%), with the highest risk seen in monochorionic twins. A global meta-analysis supports this finding, reporting an overall stillbirth prevalence of 1.4% in twin pregnancies, rising to 3.5% in monochorionic types. Fetal growth restriction, small for gestational age, and placental insufficiency are critical contributors to this risk, particularly when assessed using growth charts specific to twin gestations. [83] [84] Stillbirth risk in multiple gestations rises steeply after 37 to 38 weeks, surpassing that of post-term singleton pregnancies. Therefore, current recommendations support considering elective delivery of uncomplicated multiple gestations at 37 to 38 weeks to reduce the likelihood of stillbirth. [85]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: On rare occasions, twin reverse arterial perfusion can occur. This condition leads to an abnormal fetus with the pathophysiology of being "parasitic" to the other twin's circulation. [9] Monochorionic, monoamniotic, and twin gestations can also occur if the embryo splits after seven days. Should pathologic embryonic cleavage occur after 14 days, there is a risk for conjoined twins. Another risk unique to monochorionic twin gestations is an unequal placental mass assignment due to the unequal placement of the vascular "equator" and the intervening membrane. The fetus with a smaller placental mass assigned carries a high risk for selective fetal growth restriction. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: complications occur secondary to doubling placental mass and, thus, the associated obstetric complications of preeclampsia and gestational diabetes. Additionally, the uterus may restrict its capacity to distend and permit adequate fetal growth, thus creating a risk for preterm labor. Fetal growth restriction may also increase the risk for preterm labor and birth due to the increased risk of abnormal placentation and function. In monozygotic gestations, there is an additional unique risk of intraplacental vascular connections between the fetal circulations. Thus, the definition of chronicity in twin gestations is critical to understanding the possible obstetric risks that can develop and formulate an obstetric care plan. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Whereas the instance of dizygotic twins is variable in different populations, the prevalence of monozygotic twinning is globally constant at 3 to 5 per thousand births.  NEED REFERENCE HERE Except for post-term pregnancy and fetal macrosomia, almost all pregnancy-related risks to both the pregnant patient and the fetuses are exaggerated, accounting for a large percentage of costs to the healthcare system. Guidelines have been established for antenatal surveillance and management of twin pregnancies to improve obstetrical care and decrease perinatal complications. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The care plan and evaluation for twin gestations depend on the definition of the chorionicity of the pregnancy. [13] A routine, early imaging policy would allow diagnosis of twin gestations and establish the chronicity reliably; however, the American College Obstetricians and Gynecology do not endorse routine ultrasound examination of the abdomen in fetal low-risk pregnancies. Identifying separate placentas is a highly reliable indicator of dichorionic twins; however, a unified placental appearance may represent a single placenta and a monochorionic twin gestation, although not necessarily so.

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the larger placental mass and increased uterine contents, twin pregnancies face an elevated risk of developing gestational hypertension, preeclampsia, preterm birth, and gestational diabetes. Additionally, individuals carrying twins are more prone to complications such as deep venous thrombosis and stroke. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin-to-Twin Transfusion Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis varies depending on the stage, the severity of the disease, and gestational age at diagnosis. Younger gestational age at diagnosis and the higher stage is associated with poorer prognosis. Single-twin survival ranges from 15% to 70%, with about 50% survival of both twins, even with treatment. The prognosis is best for Stage I, with overall survival of 86%. Additionally, about 75% of Stage I remain stable or regress. [12] There is less information available for Stages II-IV, but the perinatal death rate for ≥ Stage III is estimated to be 70% to 100%. Regarding Stage V, following the demise of 1 twin, there is a 10% risk of death and a 10 to 30% risk of neurological complication in the other twin. Some research shows an improved neurological outcome in the surviving twin if fetoscopic laser photocoagulation was performed earlier in gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2371
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions “حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة” (pregnancy with quadruplets, delivered), “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية” (pregnancy with quadruplets, care episode unspecified), and “حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة” (twin pregnancy, delivered) are all classified as members of the broader category “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, which encompasses complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery while explicitly excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. This grouping indicates that both twin and higher‑order multiple pregnancies, regardless of delivery status or care‑episode specification, are considered part of the spectrum of pregnancy‑related complications and risk factors, guiding clinical monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2066
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple pregnancy (حمل متعدد), twin pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة), triplet pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة), and quadruplet pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة) are all designated as members of the Standardized MedDRA Query for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery, specifically excluding cases of miscarriage and stillbirth. This classification highlights that all forms of multiple gestation leading to live birth are recognized as clinical scenarios associated with increased obstetric complications and risk factors, underscoring the importance of careful monitoring and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is classified as several specific forms of multiple pregnancy, including حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة (twin pregnancy with delivery), حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (twin pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة (triplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة (quadruplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), and حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode). These specific pregnancy types are all children of the broader category حمل متعدد (multiple pregnancy). Both حمل متعدد and each of its constituent categories are members of مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ), the standardized query for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery that excludes miscarriage and stillbirth. This hierarchical classification links normal pregnancy and delivery to various multiple‑gestation scenarios and situates them within a recognized risk‑factor framework, facilitating comprehensive risk assessment and targeted clinical management for patients experiencing twin, triplet, or quadruplet pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized as several forms of multiple gestation, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy delivered, and quadruplet pregnancy delivered as well as quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode (noting two similar Arabic expressions for the unspecified category). These specific multiple‑pregnancy classifications are considered instances of “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy), which itself is included in the Standardized MedDRA Query “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, a grouping that captures complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery while excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. The SMQ, in turn, is part of the broader category “نواتج متبقية من الحمل” (remaining pregnancy outcomes). Consequently, each listed multiple‑pregnancy type—twin, triplet, and quadruplet pregnancies, whether delivered or unspecified for a care episode—is linked to the same complication‑risk SMQ, situating them within the overall framework of residual pregnancy outcomes and informing clinical assessment and management of normal pregnancy and delivery scenarios that involve multiple fetuses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Gestation abnormality and pregnancy and puerperal disorders are both linked to the condition ولادة قبل الأوان (preterm birth), indicating that preterm birth is classified as a specific type of gestation abnormality and also falls within the broader category of pregnancy and puerperal disorders. This dual classification underscores that preterm birth represents both an abnormality of the gestational process and a disorder affecting the pregnancy‑postpartum continuum, which is important for clinicians to recognize when assessing risk factors, planning management, and providing comprehensive care to patients experiencing or at risk for early delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases serve as clinically relevant resources for a range of obstetric conditions. They are linked to the study and management of الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes), حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (a specific type of abnormal pregnancy), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (retained products of conception), and gestation twin (twin pregnancy). By aggregating data on these entities, databases support diagnosis, risk assessment, and therapeutic decision‑making in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized into several specific multiple‑gestation types. It is classified as “حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة” (four‑fetus pregnancy that has been delivered), “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” and “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية” (four‑fetus pregnancies with care episodes that are not specified), “حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة” (triplet pregnancy that has been delivered), “حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة” (twin pregnancy that has been delivered), and “حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” (twin pregnancy with an unspecified care episode). In addition, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery has the child entity “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy). All of these entities—حمل متعدد, حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة, حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية, حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية, حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة, حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة, and حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية—are members of the SMQ “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت)” (Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery, excluding miscarriage and stillbirth). This grouping highlights that multiple gestations, whether resulting in delivery or recorded without a defined care episode, are recognized as clinical scenarios associated with increased obstetric complications and risk factors, informing patient assessment and management within the broader context of normal pregnancy and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2360
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (residual pregnancy outcomes), and gestation twin (twin gestation). These associations indicate that such databases contain relevant clinical data on the incidence, risk factors, and management of these conditions. Additionally, the low transverse cesarean section technique is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر, suggesting a link between this common surgical approach and the occurrence, diagnosis, or treatment of premature rupture of membranes. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians leverage database information to improve decision‑making and patient care in obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2924
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with gestation twin, indicating that information systems and data repositories are linked to the clinical aspects and management of twin pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 905
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized into several specific multiple‑gestation types, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy (delivered), and quadruplet pregnancy (delivered, unspecified for a care episode, and unspecified for a care episode in a slightly different wording). These multiple‑gestation categories are considered children of the broader concept “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy). All of the listed multiple‑gestation types—twin, triplet, and quadruplet pregnancies—are members of the standardized medical query “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, which groups complications and risk factors related to pregnancy, labor, and delivery while excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. In addition, the conditions “تعسر المخاض” (difficult labor) and “رأب عنق الرحم” (cervical insufficiency) are also members of this same SMQ, indicating that they are recognized as important obstetric risk factors. Postpartum obstetric operation is linked to “Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling” (care and investigation immediately after delivery), reflecting that any obstetric surgical intervention after birth requires prompt postpartum assessment and monitoring. Likewise, the relationship between “Normal pregnancy and/or delivery” and “Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling” underscores that even uncomplicated deliveries are associated with immediate postpartum care and investigation. Together, these relationships illustrate how various forms of multiple pregnancy, specific obstetric complications, and postpartum procedures are integrated within a unified framework of pregnancy‑related risk assessment and immediate post‑delivery management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: highlighting a relationship between this specific surgical technique and early‑term delivery outcomes. Preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان) is considered a child outcome of birth and is clinically associated with several factors: it is linked to the unknown cesarean‑section‑scar type (CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN), to low transverse cesarean section, and to data repositories (Databases) that capture relevant clinical information. The unknown scar type is also clinically associated with preterm birth, underscoring the importance of scar characterization in managing early deliveries. Post‑delivery residuals (نواتج متبقية من الحمل) occur after birth and are clinically associated with both birth and the Databases, indicating that these postpartum findings are documented and studied within clinical data systems. Twin gestation (gestation twin) is another condition that is clinically associated with the Databases, reflecting the need for specialized data collection in multiple‑pregnancy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (four‑twin pregnancy with unspecified care episode), حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة (triplet pregnancy delivered), حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة (twin pregnancy delivered), and حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (twin pregnancy with unspecified care episode). These categories are linked to the child concept حمل متعدد, reflecting that multiple gestations are a subcategory of normal obstetric outcomes. In the hierarchical labor terminology, LABOR AND PARTURITION is the parent of مخاض مهدد (threatened labor); مخاض قبل الأوان is clinically linked to early or threatened labor (specified as threatened preterm labor and threatened labor) and to the risk factor REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR, indicating that a poor reproductive history increases the likelihood of preterm labor. Additional conditions classified within the same SMQ include post‑delivery sepsis, placental or membrane retention without hemorrhage, other postpartum hemorrhage, fetal malposition, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: clinical data systems. Twin gestation (gestation twin) is another condition that is clinically associated with the Databases, reflecting the need for specialized data collection in multiple‑pregnancy scenarios. Overall, the clinical landscape connects birth, delivery methods (especially cesarean section and its low transverse variant), membrane integrity, gestational timing (preterm versus term), scar characteristics, contraindicating diseases such as ketorolak, and the systematic documentation of these relationships within Databases. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for optimizing obstetric care, anticipating complications, and guiding evidence‑based clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2042
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad clinical category that includes various forms of multiple‑gestation pregnancy. Within this category are specific conditions identified in different languages, such as the Czech term těhotenství s paterčaty, the Dutch phrase zwangerschap; zesling, and the Arabic expressions حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), حمل بثلاثة توائم (triplet pregnancy), and حمل بأربعة توائم (quadruplet pregnancy). These multiple‑gestation pregnancies are recognized as distinct maternal and fetal conditions because they can significantly influence labor and delivery management, requiring specialized monitoring and obstetric care to address the increased risks associated with carrying two, three, four, or more fetuses.
